definition: A fully liberated person, classically marked by the wearing away of the outflows and complete release.
notes: The project leaves arahant untranslated by default because the term functions as a precise doctrinal category and English saint brings in the wrong religious baggage. Worthy one can be used as a gloss in explanatory prose, but it should not replace the Pāli label in most source-facing work. This entry should be read as the completion point of training and the mature person-category most tightly linked to release, ending of outflows, and beyond-training status. MN 117 keeps that completion explicit by describing the arahant as ten-factored through right knowledge and right release. The OSF glossary also frames the arahant as a fully cooled-down person, which the repository treats as source-backed explanatory prose rather than a new default rendering.

context_rules.context: In most doctrinal and source-facing contexts
context_rules.notes: Leave the term untranslated by default.

context_rules.context: On first occurrence or in explanatory prose requiring a short gloss
context_rules.notes: Use as a gloss rather than a full replacement.

context_rules.context: When emphasizing completed training, worn-away outflows, or final liberation
context_rules.notes: Keep the technical person-category explicit rather than paraphrasing it away.

example_phrases.translation: an arahant whose outflows are worn away
example_phrases.notes: Shows the formula linking the category to āsava language.

authority_basis.scope: Named in notes as support for the current arahant policy.

translation_policy.default_scope: Use 'arahant' as the default rendering in the entry's governed translation contexts unless a recorded context rule overrides it.
translation_policy.when_not_to_apply: Do not force an English substitute when the local passage needs the Pali term; use a gloss only when the entry or passage explicitly calls for one.
translation_policy.drift_risk: Prevents drift back toward 'saint' or other unrecorded alternates.
translation_policy.leave_untranslated_when: Leave the term untranslated when source-facing doctrinal range or local explanation would be narrowed too quickly by an English equivalent.
